full_response:
We work in the static space-time  

ds² = −(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r² dΩ² ,         ℓ = const.

1. Conserved “energy” C  
Because the metric components are independent of t, the Killing vector ξ^a = (∂/∂t)^a implies the conserved quantity along any geodesic

E ≡ −ξ_a U^a = −g_{tt} U^t = −(−(1 + r²/ℓ²)) dt/dτ = (1 + r²/ℓ²) dt/dτ .

The problem defines  

C ≡  −(1 + r²/ℓ²) (dt/dτ) ,         so that     dt/dτ = −C /(1 + r²/ℓ²) .    (1)  

C is a constant along the geodesic.

2. Timelike normalisation  
For a timelike world-line U^a U_a = −1.  Taking, for simplicity, purely radial motion (dΩ/dτ = 0) gives  

−(1 + r²/ℓ²)(dt/dτ)² + (dr/dτ)² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = −1 .                             (2)

Insert (1):

−(1 + r²/ℓ²) [ C² /(1 + r²/ℓ²)² ] + (dr/dτ)² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = −1 ,

⇒  − C² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) + (dr/dτ)² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = −1 ,

multiply by (1 + r²/ℓ²):

−C² + (dr/dτ)² = −(1 + r²/ℓ²) ,

so

(dr/dτ)² = C² − 1 − r²/ℓ² .                                                     (3)

3. Turning point (maximum r)  
A maximum radius r_max occurs where the radial velocity momentarily vanishes,

(dr/dτ)² = 0  ⇒  C² − 1 − r_max²/ℓ² = 0 .

Hence

r_max² = ℓ²( C² − 1 ) ,        so        r_max = ℓ √(C² − 1) .                (4)

Because C is finite and (dr/dτ)² must be non-negative, eq. (3) requires

r² ≤ r_max² ,

showing that the geodesic cannot reach r = ∞.  Instead the particle oscillates between −r_max and +r_max (“boomerang” motion): gravity together with the negative cosmological constant acts like a confining box.

Condition for motion: C² ≥ 1 (C = 1 gives the trivial case r_max = 0).

\boxed{r_{\text{max}} = \ell\,\sqrt{\,C^{2}-1\,},\qquad r\le r_{\text{max}}\;,\;C^{2}\ge 1}

Confidence: 80%
